question.text: India's approach to taxation of residents and non-residents is best described as a:

answer.text: Territorial approach — both residents and non-residents are taxed only on Indian income
Universal approach — both residents and non-residents are taxed on worldwide income
Dual approach — residents are taxed on worldwide income while non-residents are taxed only on Indian income
Source-based approach — all income arising in India is taxed regardless of residential status, and foreign income is always exempt

explanation.text: **The Logic:** India follows a DUAL approach: residents (especially ROR) are taxed on their worldwide income (both Indian and foreign), while non-residents are taxed only on income that arises or is received in India. This dual system is the root cause of double taxation when two countries claim taxing rights on the same income. **The Trap:** "Territorial approach" would mean no taxation of foreign income for anyone, which is incorrect for Indian residents. "Universal approach" would mean even non-residents pay tax on worldwide income, which is wrong. "Source-based" oversimplifies the framework. **Cross-Exam Context:** The dual approach concept is foundational to understanding DTAA and is tested across NISM Series X-A and X-B. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The dual approach means an Indian resident investing abroad faces potential double taxation — always structure advice with DTAA relief in mind.
